seed_entries.title: How to turn AI research into useful articles
seed_entries.excerpt: A practical editorial workflow for converting collected AI notes, source data, and product observations into articles readers can act on.
seed_entries.body:
Useful AI writing starts before generation. Keep source notes, claims, examples, and product observations separate, then decide what problem the article helps a reader solve.

A strong workflow has four steps: collect evidence, cluster it into a clear angle, draft with an AI assistant, and require human review before publishing. The AI module can support drafting, but the content system should own status, site scope, and final publishing.

This keeps the platform flexible. Data from projects such as research workspaces can become source material without forcing every future AI provider or plugin into one fixed implementation.

seed_entries.title: What an AI platform needs beyond a blog
seed_entries.excerpt: Why a long-term AI site should treat articles, products, tools, and AI-generated drafts as connected platform objects.
seed_entries.body:
A blog page is useful, but a durable AI platform needs a content system. Articles should be site-scoped, status-aware, searchable by topic, and able to connect back to digital products.

That structure lets the same backend support articles, guides, reports, reviews, and resource pages. It also gives AI plugins a safe place to write drafts without publishing directly to readers.

The practical target is not a perfect CMS on day one. The target is a reusable content core, a human-friendly admin surface, and frontend pages that can improve as real publishing habits emerge.

seed_entries.title: Provider-neutral AI is the right default
seed_entries.excerpt: OpenRouter, OpenAI, Claude, local gateways, or future providers can all fit if AI configuration is separated from concrete task plugins.
seed_entries.body:
AI infrastructure changes quickly, so a store platform should avoid hard-coding one provider. The stable layer is configuration: provider code, protocol, endpoint, model, key reference, site scope, and readiness status.

Specific features should be plugins. One plugin may draft articles, another may summarize product data, and another may help support. Each plugin can choose a provider through the AI core instead of embedding provider assumptions.

This separation keeps deployment flexible and gives operators a clear backend panel for health, missing keys, review queues, and future AI work.

guardrails: Keep checkout, cart, order lookup, and delivery access consistent with the shared storefront.
Use content and product sections to support trust without turning the site into a marketing-only page.
Prefer profile-driven section order and variants before adding one-off page code.

sections.goal: Explain the AI product promise and route users to catalog or order lookup.
Use articles and guides to build trust before product browsing.
Show purchasable AI products through the shared commerce card system.
